content.text: What it is

Short-lived branches for agentic AI application teams is the practice of keeping every feature branch open for fewer than two days before merging to the main trunk. Borrowed from trunk-based development, the constraint is tightened for AI teams because the artefacts most prone to merge conflict — system prompts, few-shot example sets, tool schemas, and evaluation datasets — are prose or structured data files that do not benefit from automated merge tools and frequently require manual conflict resolution. The longer a branch stays open, the higher the probability that a parallel change to the same prompt file or tool schema creates an unsatisfiable conflict.

A short-lived branch workflow structures work so that each pull request represents the smallest meaningful unit of AI behaviour change: one prompt constraint addition, one tool argument change, one few-shot example category update. This decomposition keeps branch lifetime short, makes each change reviewable in isolation, and ensures that the evaluation suite runs against small diffs where regressions are easy to attribute to a specific change.

attrs.alt: Short-lived branch lifecycle for GenAI changes: fork from trunk, commit prompt edits over 1-3 days, squash-merge back

content.text: Why Short-Lived Branches Are a Safety Property

Short-lived branches are not merely a code hygiene preference for GenAI work — they are a safety property. A system prompt that lives in a long-running branch for two weeks is a system prompt that has not been reviewed, has not had its eval suite run against main's current state, and may be merged with stale context about what the production system actually does. The longer a GenAI branch lives, the greater the divergence from trunk, and the greater the risk that a merge introduces a behavioral regression that neither CI nor code review will catch.

The fundamental discipline is: a GenAI branch should exist only long enough to complete one well-scoped change, pass CI, and receive ensemble review. Three days is a common hard limit for prompt changes. Five days for integration layer changes. If a branch is still open after those periods, the scope was too large, or the team did not prioritize the review.
Branch Naming and Scope Locking

Every GenAI branch must communicate its type and scope in its name. The naming convention (<type>/<jira-id>-<short-description>) makes it immediately visible in a branch list whether a branch is a prompt change, an eval suite change, an integration layer change, or an agent wiring change. Mixing types on one branch is prohibited — it causes the Safety Reviewer to reject the PR because they cannot isolate what behavioral change they are approving.

Scope locking means: the branch scope is fixed at creation time. If you discover during a prompt change that you also need to update the tool call wiring, you open a separate branch for that change and coordinate the order of merges. This constraint feels slow in the short term and saves significant time in debugging production regressions.

# genai-branch-policy.md
# Short-Lived Branch Policy for GenAI Prompt and Integration Changes
# Version: 1.0  Owner: AI Platform Team

## Branch Naming Convention

All GenAI-related branches must follow this naming scheme:

  <type>/<jira-id>-<short-description>

Where <type> is one of:
  - prompt/    System prompt or instruction changes
  - eval/      Eval suite additions or fixes
  - integ/     Integration layer changes (LLM client, tool calls)
  - agent/     Agent wiring or orchestration changes

Examples:
  prompt/AIP-214-tighten-refusal-rules
  eval/AIP-219-add-pii-leak-cases
  integ/AIP-231-switch-to-streaming

## Lifetime Limits

| Change Type    | Hard Limit | SLA     |
|----------------|------------|---------|
| prompt/        | 3 days     | 2 days  |
| eval/          | 2 days     | 1 day   |
| integ/         | 5 days     | 3 days  |
| agent/         | 5 days     | 3 days  |

Branches that exceed the hard limit must be discussed in a team standup
before any new commits are made. Long-running branches signal scope creep.

## Scope Rules

A prompt/ branch may contain:
  - Changes to system prompt files in prompts/
  - Changes to prompt version metadata (prompt-registry.json)
  - Eval suite additions covering the new prompt behaviour
  - Documentation (CHANGELOG.md, prompt-notes/)

A prompt/ branch must NOT contain:
  - New tool definitions or tool call wiring
  - Schema changes to the LLM client configuration
  - Model parameter changes (temperature, max_tokens)
  - Feature flag additions or removals

Mixing concerns causes the Safety Reviewer to reject the PR outright.

## Merge Requirements

Before merging to main, every GenAI branch must have:
  [ ] CI green on the full eval suite
  [ ] Safety Reviewer approval
  [ ] PM/Product approval (Major or Safety classification)
  [ ] Eng Lead approval (Safety or agent/ branches)
  [ ] Prompt hash updated in prompt-registry.json
  [ ] CHANGELOG entry with change class and motivation

## Squash Merge Policy

All GenAI branches are squash-merged into main. The squash commit message
must follow Conventional Commits format:

  feat(prompt): <description> [AIP-<id>]

  - Change class: <minor|major|safety>
  - Prompt hash: <sha256 of new prompt file>
  - Eval delta: <pass/fail counts before → after>
  - Reviewed by: @<safety-reviewer>, @<pm-reviewer>

## Stale Branch Cleanup

A GitHub Action checks for GenAI branches older than their hard limit
every morning at 08:00 UTC and posts a Slack alert to #ai-platform.
Branches are not force-deleted — the author must act.
Branch Lifecycle Management Script

The following script enforces branch naming conventions, computes branch age against policy limits, and provides a create command that sets up the branch correctly from main. Run 'check-age' in CI or as a pre-push hook to prevent developers from continuing work on branches past their limit.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# branch_lifecycle.py
# Create and manage short-lived GenAI branches according to policy
# Usage:
#   python branch_lifecycle.py create --type prompt --jira AIP-214 --desc tighten-refusal-rules
#   python branch_lifecycle.py check-age
#   python branch_lifecycle.py list

import subprocess
import sys
import re
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Optional

BRANCH_POLICY = {
    "prompt": {"hard_limit_days": 3, "sla_days": 2},
    "eval":   {"hard_limit_days": 2, "sla_days": 1},
    "integ":  {"hard_limit_days": 5, "sla_days": 3},
    "agent":  {"hard_limit_days": 5, "sla_days": 3},
}

GENAI_BRANCH_PATTERN = re.compile(
    r"^(prompt|eval|integ|agent)/([A-Z]+-\d+)-(.+)$"
)

@dataclass
class BranchInfo:
    name: str
    branch_type: str
    jira_id: str
    description: str
    created_at: datetime
    age_days: float

def run(cmd: list[str], check: bool = True) -> str:
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, check=check)
    return result.stdout.strip()

def get_branch_created_at(branch: str) -> Optional[datetime]:
    # Returns the datetime of the first commit on the branch not on main.
    try:
        log = run(["git", "log", "main.." + branch, "--format=%aI", "--reverse"])
        if not log:
            return None
        first_line = log.splitlines()[0]
        return datetime.fromisoformat(first_line)
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError:
        return None

def list_genai_branches() -> List[BranchInfo]:
    branches_raw = run(["git", "branch", "--format=%(refname:short)"])
    results = []
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    for branch in branches_raw.splitlines():
        m = GENAI_BRANCH_PATTERN.match(branch)
        if not m:
            continue
        btype, jira, desc = m.group(1), m.group(2), m.group(3)
        created_at = get_branch_created_at(branch)
        if not created_at:
            continue
        age_days = (now - created_at).total_seconds() / 86400
        results.append(BranchInfo(branch, btype, jira, desc, created_at, age_days))
    return results

def check_age(branches: List[BranchInfo]) -> None:
    print(f"{'Branch':<45} {'Type':<8} {'Age':>6} {'SLA':>5} {'Limit':>7} {'Status'}")
    print("-" * 85)
    any_over = False
    for b in branches:
        policy = BRANCH_POLICY[b.branch_type]
        sla = policy["sla_days"]
        limit = policy["hard_limit_days"]
        if b.age_days > limit:
            status = "OVER LIMIT"
            any_over = True
        elif b.age_days > sla:
            status = "past SLA"
        else:
            status = "ok"
        print(f"{b.name:<45} {b.branch_type:<8} {b.age_days:>5.1f}d {sla:>4}d {limit:>6}d  {status}")
    if any_over:
        print("\nWARNING: branches over hard limit must be discussed in standup before new commits.")
        sys.exit(1)

def create_branch(btype: str, jira: str, desc: str) -> None:
    if btype not in BRANCH_POLICY:
        print(f"Unknown type: {btype}. Must be one of: {list(BRANCH_POLICY.keys())}")
        sys.exit(1)
    branch_name = f"{btype}/{jira}-{desc}"
    if not re.match(r'^[a-z0-9/-]+$', branch_name.replace(jira.upper(), jira).lower()):
        print("Branch name contains invalid characters. Use lowercase letters, digits, hyphens, slashes only.")
        sys.exit(1)
    run(["git", "checkout", "main"])
    run(["git", "pull", "origin", "main"])
    run(["git", "checkout", "-b", branch_name])
    print(f"Created branch: {branch_name}")
    policy = BRANCH_POLICY[btype]
    print(f"SLA: {policy['sla_days']}d | Hard limit: {policy['hard_limit_days']}d")
    print("Remember: scope is locked at branch creation. Add no other change types.")

def main() -> None:
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="GenAI branch lifecycle manager")
    sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="cmd", required=True)

    create_p = sub.add_parser("create", help="Create a new GenAI branch")
    create_p.add_argument("--type", required=True, choices=list(BRANCH_POLICY.keys()))
    create_p.add_argument("--jira", required=True)
    create_p.add_argument("--desc", required=True)

    sub.add_parser("check-age", help="Check age of all GenAI branches")
    sub.add_parser("list", help="List all GenAI branches")

    args = parser.parse_args()
    if args.cmd == "create":
        create_branch(args.type, args.jira, args.desc)
    elif args.cmd in ("check-age", "list"):
        branches = list_genai_branches()
        if not branches:
            print("No GenAI branches found.")
            return
        check_age(branches)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
GitHub Actions: Stale Branch Alert

A daily GitHub Action sweeps all GenAI branches and posts a Slack alert if any are past their hard limit. This creates ambient accountability without requiring manual monitoring. The alert does not force-delete branches — it notifies the author and the team so they can decide whether to merge, abandon, or discuss the scope in standup.

# .github/workflows/stale-genai-branches.yml
# Checks for GenAI branches past their hard lifetime limit
# Runs at 08:00 UTC daily and posts a Slack alert

name: Stale GenAI Branch Alert

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 8 * * 1-5"   # Monday–Friday at 08:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  check-stale:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0      # full history needed to compute branch ages

      - name: Check branch ages
        id: age-check
        run: |
          python3 scripts/branch_lifecycle.py check-age > stale_report.txt 2>&1 || true
          echo "report<<EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          cat stale_report.txt >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          if grep -q "OVER LIMIT" stale_report.txt; then
            echo "has_over=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          else
            echo "has_over=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi

      - name: Post Slack alert
        if: steps.age-check.outputs.has_over == 'true'
        uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1.27
        with:
          payload: |
            {
              "text": ":rotating_light: *Stale GenAI Branch Alert*\n```${{ steps.age-check.outputs.report }}```\nPlease merge, abandon, or discuss in standup."
            }
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.SLACK_AI_PLATFORM_WEBHOOK }}
What Makes a GenAI Branch Hard to Keep Short

The most common reason GenAI branches grow long is that the initial scope was not well-defined. A ticket that says 'improve the agent's refusal behaviour' is not a branch scope — it is a project. A branch scope is a single, testable behavioral change: 'add a refusal rule for requests that ask the agent to impersonate a human'. When the scope is that specific, the branch almost always closes within two days.
Prompt and eval changes grow together: a prompt change that requires new eval cases is fine. A prompt change that requires a new eval framework is two branches. Separate the framework work first.

Waiting for review extends branch lifetime: if ensemble review is a three-day wait, branches will be open for five days regardless of how fast the author works. Fix review SLAs before tightening branch lifetime limits.

Integration dependencies block merges: if a prompt change depends on a new tool that is not yet merged to main, the prompt branch must wait. Use feature flags to decouple the dependency, not a long-running branch.

Scope creep from discovered side effects: when testing a prompt change, developers discover a related issue and fix it on the same branch. Discipline requires opening a separate branch for the discovered fix and proceeding with the original scope only.

Incomplete eval coverage delays confidence: if the eval suite does not cover the behavior the branch changes, developers feel they cannot merge because they are not sure the change is correct. Writing evals first (eval-driven development) eliminates this delay.
Abandoning a Branch

A branch that has grown beyond its scope or that has been superseded by a different approach should be abandoned, not continued. Abandoning a branch means: close the PR (if open) with a comment explaining the reason, delete the branch, and create a new branch with the correct scope. Do not try to rescue a scope-creep branch by reverting commits — the history becomes unreadable and the Safety Reviewer cannot determine what the net change is.
Close the PR with comment: 'Abandoning — scope exceeded policy. New branch: <branch-name>.'
Delete the remote branch: git push origin --delete <branch-name>
Delete the local branch: git branch -D <branch-name>
Open a new branch from the latest main with the correct scope.
If the abandoned branch contained useful prompt changes, cherry-pick only the prompt file commit into the new branch — do not copy the entire branch.
Relationship to Trunk Stability

Short-lived branches exist to serve trunk stability, not to create process overhead. Every merge to main is a deployment candidate. If a prompt change is on a branch for five days and trunk has moved forward, the merge may introduce a conflict between the new prompt and a model parameter change that was merged to trunk on day three. Short branches minimize the merge window and reduce the probability of silent behavioral conflicts.

The practical rhythm for a healthy GenAI team is: create branch, implement change the same day, run evals, open PR the same day or next morning, receive ensemble review within 24 hours, merge by end of day two. This rhythm requires that ensemble reviewers treat prompt PRs as high-priority interrupts, not backlog items. The branch lifetime limit and the review SLA are jointly enforced properties — one without the other breaks the system.
Benefits
Reduced prompt merge conflicts: branches open for less than two days rarely compete with other prompt changes at the per-day timescale.
Faster eval feedback: small diffs make it obvious which specific change caused a regression rather than attributing it to a large multi-commit batch.
Smaller review surface: ensemble reviewers (safety, product) can review a one-constraint prompt change in minutes rather than hours.
Continuous trunk health: with small, frequent merges the trunk always reflects a working state, making it safe to cut a release at any point.
Trade-offs

Short-lived branches require feature flags for every incomplete multi-step feature: a partial prompt change that is not ready for user exposure must be guarded by a flag before it can merge to trunk. This creates additional flag management overhead. Teams that adopt this discipline discover that their feature flag management process improves as a side-effect, since flags become first-class artefacts rather than ad-hoc code comments.
Feature flag overhead: every incomplete AI feature needs a flag before its first short branch can merge; this adds a setup step to the start of each feature.
More pull requests: decomposing features into short branches produces more PRs per feature, each requiring ensemble review if they touch prompt or tool schema files.
Sequential dependency management: prompt changes that depend on each other cannot merge simultaneously; the team must sequence them, which requires upfront planning.
When to use it
Any team with two or more engineers contributing to the same system prompt or tool schema file, where parallel long-lived branches reliably produce conflicts.
Projects under active prompt improvement cycles where frequent small changes need rapid eval-suite feedback rather than batch evaluation of large diffs.
Regulated AI applications where every prompt change must have a complete audit trail of individual review decisions.
Best practices
Decompose work at the behaviour level: one branch per prompt constraint, one branch per tool argument change, one branch per example category addition.
Open a draft PR immediately when creating a branch: this gives the team visibility into in-flight prompt changes and prevents two engineers from editing the same section simultaneously.
Run the eval suite on every push to the branch, not just on the final PR state, so regressions are caught before review begins.
Set a branch age gate in CI: warn at two days, block merge at four days. This makes branch age visible without being immediately blocking during the transition period.
Write a two-to-five-line decomposition note before cutting the first branch for a multi-step feature, listing the planned merge units and their ordering constraints.
Security and reliability

Short-lived branches reduce the window during which an in-progress prompt change can interact with a security vulnerability. Long-lived branches that contain a prompt injection mitigation may remain unmerged for two weeks while other security patches land on trunk, creating a divergent security posture. Short branches ensure that prompt security changes reach trunk quickly and are evaluated in the CI eval suite against the current model rather than a two-week-old model state.
Key takeaways
Short-lived branches (under two days) prevent the prompt and tool schema merge conflicts that are uniquely damaging in agentic AI projects because those files cannot be auto-merged.
Feature flags are a prerequisite: they allow incomplete features to merge to trunk safely without being user-facing.
Decompose work at the behaviour level — one constraint, one argument, one example category — to keep branch scope small enough to merge within two days.
A branch age gate in CI (warning at 2 days, block at 4) enforces the discipline without requiring constant manual enforcement.
Consistent branch naming conventions (prompt/, tool/, eval/, feat/) enable automatic CI routing and reviewer assignment based on the type of AI artefact being changed.
